Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name, sum(Revenue) FROM Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID AND Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID AND City = 'Nijmegen' AND Year = 2021 GROUP BY Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Customer_DIM, Fact_Table, Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.Customer_ID = Fact_Table.Customer_ID AND Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.time_ID AND Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name ;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table JOIN Customer_DIM ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM ON Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID WHERE Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table f JOIN Customer_DIM c ON f.Customer_ID = c.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM t ON f.Time_ID = t.Time_ID WHERE c.City = 'Nijmegen'    AND t.Year = 2021 GROUP BY c.Name

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table F NATURAL JOIN Customer_DIM C NATURAL JOIN Time_DIM T WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT F.Name,SUM(F.Revenue) FROM Fact_Table AS F, Customer_DIM AS C, Time_DIM AS T WHERE F.Customer_ID=C.Customer_ID AND F.Time_ID=T.Time_ID AND C.City='Nijmegen' AND T.Year=2021 GROUP BY F.Customer_ID

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT C.Name AND SUM(F.Revenue)  FROM Customer_DIM C AND Fact_Table F AND Time_DIM T  WHERE T.Time_ID = F.Time_ID AND C.Customer_ID = F.Customer_ID AND C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Customer_DIM, Fact_Table, Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.City='Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM,Year='2021' AND Customer_DIM.Customer_ID=Fact_Table.Customer_ID AND Time_DIM.Time_ID=Fact_Table.Time_ID GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Answer: SELECT Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Customer_DIM, Fact_Table, Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.Customer_ID = Fact_Table.Customer_ID  AND  Time_DIM.Time_ID = Fact_Table.Time_ID AND  Time_DIM.Year = '2021' AND Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' GROUP BY Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT Name, SUM(Revenue) AS Customer_Revenue FROM Fact_Table NATURAL JOIN Customer_Dim NATURAL JOIN Time_DIM  WHERE Customer_DIM.City = "Nijmegen" AND Time_DIM.Year= "2021" GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name,SUM(Revenue) FROM Customer_DIM,Fact_Table,Time_DIM WHERE Year='2021' AND City='Nijmegen' AND Customer_DIM.Customer_ID=Fact_Table.CustomerID AND Time_DIM.Time_ID=Fact_Table.Time_ID;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name, Revenue FROM Customer_DIM C, Fact_Table F, Time_DIM T WHERE F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID AND T.Year = 2021 AND C.Customer_ID = F.Customer_ID AND C.City = "Nijmegen" ;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) FROM Customer_DIM NATURAL JOIN Fact_Table NATURAL JOIN Time_DIM WHERE Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Fact_Table.Customer ID;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT DISTINCT C.Name AS Customer_Name, SUM(F.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen'  AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) AS TotalRevenue FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name,SUM(Revenue) FROM Fact_Table JOIN Customer_DIM ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID=Customer_DIM.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM ON Fact_Table.Time_ID=Time_DIM.Time_ID WHERE City='Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.year=2021 GROUP BY Name;

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table  JOIN Customer_DIM ON Customer_DIM.Customer_ID = Fact_Table.Customer_ID  JOIN Time_DIM ON Time_DIM.Time_ID = Fact_Table.Time_ID WHERE Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = '2021' GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name AS Customer_Name, SUM(F.Revenue) AS Total_Revenue FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Answer: SELECT c.Name, SUM(f.Revenue) FROM 'CUSTOMER DIM' c LEFT JOIN 'FACT TABLE' f ON c.'Customer ID' = f.'Customer ID' GROUP BY c.Name;

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB prepares for a ROLLBACK or similar error during loading. In this case, discarding of the transaction-local state is free and optimistic.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) AS TotalRevenue FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name, SUM (Revenue) FROM Fact_Table, Customer_DIM, Time_DIM WHERE Fact_Table.Customer_ID=Customer_DIM.Customer_ID AND Fact_Table.Time_ID=Time_DIM.Time_ID AND City='Nijmegen' AND Year=2021 GROUP BY Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for finding a single table entry, by optimistically creating secondary indexes for primary keys and columns with uniqueness constraints. When the transaction commits, those indexes will already be created on disk.

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Customer_DIM.Name, SUM(Fact_Table.Revenue) as total_Revenue  FROM Customer_DIM, Fact_Table  JOIN Customer_DIM ON Fact_Table.Customer_ID = Customer_DIM.Customer_ID  JOIN Time_DIM ON Fact_Table.Time_ID = Time_DIM.Time_ID  WHERE Customer_DIM.City = 'Nijmegen' AND Time_DIM.Year = 2021 GROUP BY Customer_DIM.Name;

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, T.Quarter, SUM(F.Revenue) AS TotalRevenue FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON F.Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

When the transaction is committed, the data will already have been written to disk, and no further writing has to happen.  On a ROLLBACK, any optimistically written data is reclaimed by the system. Why is this strategy for persisting data called "optimistic"?
Answer: DuckDB optimizes for many small transactions. These transactions will all be optimistically written (assuming a ROLLBACK will not occur) to the database file, so the intermediate states do not have to be stored in temporary files.

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT C.Name, SUM(F.Revenue) AS TotalRevenue FROM Fact_Table F JOIN Customer_DIM C ON F.Customer_ID = C.Customer_ID JOIN Time_DIM T ON Time_ID = T.Time_ID WHERE C.City = 'Nijmegen' AND T.Year = 2021 GROUP BY C.Name;

Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. When loading large data sets in a single transaction, data is compressed and streamed to the database file, even before the COMMIT has occurred. Q Text: Read the following fragment from the blog post introducing DuckDB version 6: This version introduces optimistic writing to disk. Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Cust.Name, SUM(Fact.Revenue) FROM Customer_DIM Cust, Time_DIM Time, Fact_Table Fact WHERE Fact.Customer_ID == Cust.Customer_ID AND Cust.City == 'Nijmegen' AND Fact.Time_ID == Time.Time_ID AND Time.Year == 2021 GROUP BY Cust.Name;

Answer: DuckDB optimizes for loading large datasets, and will not be able to hold the transaction-local state in main memory. Instead of writing intermediate data to temporary files, it optimistically writes them to the database file (assuming a ROLLBACK is rare).

Q Text: Given the following star schema with a fact table and dimension tables: Fact_Table(Product_ID, Time_ID, Promotion_ID, Customer_ID, Revenue, Units_Sold)Product_DIM(Product_ID, Product_Name, Unit_Price, Product_Line)Customer_DIM(Customer_ID, Name, City, Zip)Time_DIM(Time_ID, Order_Date, Month, Quarter, Year)Promotion_DIM(Promotion_ID, Promo_Name, Ad_Type, Coupon_Type, Price_Reduction_Type) Primary keys are underlined. The schema is also shown in the image below:  Give the SQL query that returns the Name and total Revenue for Customers in the City of 'Nijmegen' in the Year 2021.
Answer: SELECT Name, Revenue FROM 'CUSTOMER DIM' JOIN 'FACT TABLE' USING cin GROUP BY Name
